focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The rental car was a gray Hyundai Elantra that smelled like the previous renter's choices: a pine air freshener clipped to the vent, dried coffee in the cupholder, the stale salt-and-grease trace of a fast food meal that had happened somewhere between here and wherever that person had been going. Layla had booked the tour in a single phone call while Doug was on his lunch break. Cleveland Thursday, Pittsburgh Friday, Detroit Saturday. "Warm-ups," she called them. "Before the big show." The Netflix taping was in two weeks.

She had emailed him a PDF itinerary with club addresses, hotel confirmations, and a note in all caps that said DO NOT OVERTHINK THIS. Doug had printed it because he did not trust phone batteries. The pages sat on the passenger seat beside a yellow legal pad with his set list divided into blocks: OPENING, INSURANCE, CEREAL, DIVORCE, CLOSER. Under OPENING he had written two possible first lines and circled the one he disliked less. Under CLOSER he had written LAND HERE, as if landing were a matter of instruction.

He pulled onto I-71 north toward Cleveland and did not feel warm.

He had driven this route to visit Kelsey when she first moved there, before the visits became less frequent, then occasional, then something they planned over text three weeks in advance. Delaware. Mansfield. Ashland. He knew where the highway widened and where it narrowed, where the rest stop coffee started tasting less like Columbus and more like trucks. Kelsey lived twenty minutes from the club. He had not told her he was coming. That felt less cruel than telling her and making her decide whether to see him bomb in person.

The Holiday Inn was off an exit lined with chain restaurants that had all decided on different versions of beige. The lobby smelled like le...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...his desk. His coffee was half-drunk.

"I read the headline," Roy said.

"Just the headline?"

"It was four words. That's three too many." Roy took a sip of his coffee. "They called you a comedian."

Doug waited.

"You gave one speech, Doug. You were drunk."

Roy turned back to his screen.

Doug looked at his own. Fourteen claims. A burst pipe in Westerville. A chimney fire in Dublin. A woman in Upper Arlington whose sump pump had failed during last week's rain. She'd photographed her basement from four angles. She'd kept the receipt for the pump. Her handwritten description got tighter and more frantic in the margins where she'd added what she'd remembered later.

Janet forwarded three phone messages from reception because people kept asking for him by full name. One was from the fatherhood podcast. One was from the Denver retreat. One was from a college paper whose reporter wanted to know whether the second bedroom was "still unresolved." The second bedroom was full of boxes. The article had been read more carefully than the apartment had.

The desk phone's red light blinked through the morning. Layla, probably, or a producer, or somebody who wanted the man who bought symbolic cereal and lived beside meaningful boxes. That version of Doug was already easier to book than the real one. He fit in a subject line. He fit in a panel theme. He fit in the clean white space next to a professional headshot.

Doug opened the Upper Arlington file. Coverage applied. No exclusions. He wrote the assessment, entered the determination code, and moved to the next screen.

The phone kept blinking. Doug picked it up. It was Layla. He put her on hold and dialed the woman in Upper Arlington. She picked up on the second ring and immediately started apologizing for the water in her basement.
